agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Real-time courier tracking feature for customer-facing delivery experience. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, Operations demands no location sharing. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved at the technical level — it requires executive arbitration.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer Experience","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver/delivery person fulfilling a delivery order"},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"GPS coordinates of the courier's device, updated continuously during delivery"},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to a customer"},{"term":"Location sharing","definition":"The act of transmitting courier GPS data to the customer-facing application"},{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated time of arrival — a derived value that can communicate delivery progress without exposing raw location"}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier turns off GPS or loses signal during delivery","expected_behavior":"System must degrade gracefully regardless of which location-sharing policy is chosen — stale data is worse than no data"},{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, lunch break)","expected_behavior":"Raw coordinates would expose this to customers, creating complaints. An abstracted status would not."},{"scenario":"Courier delivers to sensitive locations (domestic violence shelters, medical facilities)","expected_behavior":"Even if location sharing is enabled, reverse-path inference from delivery sequences could expose courier patterns — privacy risk exists in both directions"},{"scenario":"Customer claims non-delivery but courier GPS shows arrival","expected_behavior":"Internal GPS logging for dispute resolution is a separate concern from customer-facing visibility — both teams may agree on internal use"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High. Customer Success claims transparency drives retention and reduces support costs. Operations claims driver churn is increasing, and replacing drivers costs $3,000-$5,000 per hire in the courier industry. Both sides have legitimate financial arguments that need data, not assertions.","operational":"Critical. Driver retention directly affects delivery capacity and SLA compliance. If Operations is correct that drivers are leaving over surveillance concerns, showing location could degrade the very service customers want to track.","user":"Dual-sided. Customers want delivery predictability. Couriers want workplace dignity. Both are users of the platform. Optimizing for one at the direct expense of the other is not sustainable.","risk":"Regulatory risk: real-time worker surveillance may trigger labor law concerns in certain jurisdictions (EU GDPR, California CCPA, emerging gig-worker protection laws). Legal review is mandatory before any location-sharing policy is finalized.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery visibility — but may be equally satisfied with ETA/status updates rather than raw GPS coordinates"},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected by surveillance concerns — retention and morale at stake"},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Measured on customer satisfaction and support ticket volume — needs a solution that delivers predictability"},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Measured on driver retention, delivery capacity, and operational costs — needs driver trust preserved"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Not yet consulted — must weigh in on worker surveillance regulations before any implementation"},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict — it is a strategic business decision, not a feature decision"}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Confidence is critically low because two authoritative stakeholder teams have issued mutually exclusive non-negotiable requirements. This is not a technical ambiguity — it is a business strategy conflict. No amount of domain analysis can resolve a direct contradiction between two teams with equal authority. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not been consulted on worker surveillance implications, which could override both positions. The spec as given is unimplementable.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine contradiction that cannot be resolved by clever technical design alone","Both teams have legitimate business concerns grounded in real operational data","There are well-known industry patterns (ETA-based tracking, zone-based tracking) that may offer compromise positions","Legal review is mandatory before any location-sharing feature ships"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which team's priority should take precedence — this is an executive decision","Whether a compromise (e.g., ETA-only, zone-based) would satisfy Customer Success","Whether driver attrition is actually caused by location tracking or correlated with other factors","Regulatory requirements in the company's operating jurisdictions"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith based on real signals from their constituents","No executive decision has already been made that supersedes one team's position","The company operates in jurisdictions where worker location tracking has regulatory implications"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers as a non-negotiable transparency feature. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers as a non-negotiable driver-retention measure. These requirements are mutually exclusive and cannot both be satisfied. This is a business strategy decision that must be escalated to executive leadership — it cannot be resolved at the feature team level.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers/Drivers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Legal/Compliance","Executive leadership"],"options":["A: Full GPS transparency (Customer Success wins, Operations loses — risk driver attrition)","B: No location sharing (Operations wins, Customer Success loses — risk customer dissatisfaction)","C: Compromise via abstracted tracking — show ETA, delivery status, or zone-level proximity instead of raw GPS. This is the industry-standard pattern (UberEats, DoorDash show approximate location, not exact coordinates). Requires both teams to accept a middle ground.","D: Consent-based model — couriers opt in to location sharing, customers see ETA-only for non-consenting couriers. Respects driver autonomy but creates inconsistent customer experience."],"recommendation":"Option C (abstracted tracking) is the industry-proven approach and likely satisfies the core needs of both teams: customers get delivery predictability without raw surveillance, drivers get dignity without being invisible. However, this recommendation CANNOT be accepted by an engineer or PM — it must be ratified by executive leadership with both teams present. Legal must also review before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Contradictory requirements from two authoritative stakeholder teams","business_rule_broken":"Cannot simultaneously show and not-show courier location. Implementation is blocked until executive arbitration resolves the conflict."}]}
